body_txt: This trope is Truth in Television for naked mole rats as they are one of the few rodents with visible incisors when their mouth is closed. Although some rodents, including pocket gophers and the aforementioned naked mole rats, do in fact have protruding teeth, in real life these are usually hidden by the skin and muscles of the mouth, and therefore are not visible. This trope is even less true of Real Life lagomorphs than real life rodents. Female rodents and lagomorphs are often depicted with smaller protruding buck teeth or none at all as a Tertiary Sexual Characteristic to distinguish them from their male counterparts. Among rodents, this trait is least commonly depicted in cartoon porcupines, chinchillas, guinea pigs, and capybaras. Those buck teeth are also generally white in color, unlike in some rodents in real life. For example, real life beavers and nutria/coypus have yellow orange teeth. Sometimes the character in question isn't even a rodent or lagomorph note Rodents and lagomorphs are grouped within Glires. at all, but still has long teeth for no discernible reason or has them due to having a rodent or lagomorph motif. Can overlap with Goofy Buckteeth if the rodent is also rather dorky or geeky/nerdy. Subtrope of Animal Stereotypes . Compare Asian Buck Teeth , in which the title teeth are part of Asian stereotypes. No Real Life Examples, Please!
